Defendant Crane Co.'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Added To Calendar For The Entry Of The Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Feb-02-2017 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-02-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Kimberly J. Chu of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiff / opposing party James Roscoe

RULING:
DEFENDANT CRANE CO.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

No other appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Crane Co.' motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication are denied. As to summary judgment and summary adjudication issues nos. 1-4, defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that he was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant. Plaintiff's deposition testimony (as to the internal packing in Crane valves) and responses to defendant's special interrogatories preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Even if defendant did sustain its initial burden, plaintiff's deposition testimony and the testimony of William McLean and Anthony Pantaleoni (Exhibits E, F, G) create a triable issue whether plaintiff was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials (internal packing) attributable to defendant. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that exposure to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant was a substantial factor in causing or contributing to his increased risk of developing his asbestos-related illness. Plaintiff's responses to defendant's special discovery preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. As to summary adjudication issue no. 5 (punitive damages), defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, clear and convincing evidence that defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. Plaintiff's responses to defendant's special interrogatories preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Even if defendant sustained its initial burden, plaintiff's evidence (Exhibits C, E, G, H, N, P and X) creates a triable issue whether defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. While the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration, plaintiff does not need to prove his case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage and the Court cannot conclude that no reasonable jury could find plaintiff's evidence to be clear and convincing proof of malice, fraud or oppression. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, matter not reported (503/CML)
